does: This paper introduces sparse crosscoders, a method for discovering and representing consistent features that appear across multiple layers within a neural network, and potentially across different models. The approach builds on sparse autoencoders by extending them to operate across layer boundaries, using sparsity-inducing constraints to identify interpretable feature dimensions that maintain coherence despite the different representational spaces at each layer. The method enables systematic comparison of internal representations across depth and model boundaries through a unified feature vocabulary.
connects: This work extends the sparse autoencoder framework developed for mechanistic interpretability, which has become central to understanding individual layer representations. It responds to the challenge that features identified in one layer often do not align cleanly with those in adjacent layers, limiting our ability to track how computations flow through networks. The paper builds on prior work examining polysemanticity and feature splitting across layers, and relates to emerging efforts in model diffing—comparing the internal structure of different models to understand convergent learning dynamics. It connects to broader mechanistic interpretability research seeking systematic ways to dissect neural computation.
enables: This approach opens pathways for several future directions: tracing how abstract concepts evolve and transform across network depth, constructing universal feature bases that could apply across model families, and developing principled model comparison techniques that go beyond behavioral similarity. The work could support research into understanding when and why models learn similar internal representations, whether certain computational strategies are universal, and how to transfer interpretability knowledge between models. Sparse crosscoders may facilitate debugging neural networks and discovering conserved algorithmic structures across architectural variations.
